description: Control-theory grounding for the robotics + ml lenses — the standout thin-on-deep pair. Each entry is a crisp, accurate knowledge DTU (definition + the load-bearing claim) that grounds reasoning in those lenses instead of leaving them to search a flat pool.

entries.title: PID control
entries.summary: A PID controller drives a system toward a setpoint using the error e(t): u = Kp·e + Ki·∫e dt + Kd·de/dt. Proportional reacts to present error, integral erases steady-state offset, derivative damps overshoot by anticipating. Ziegler–Nichols gives a first-cut tuning; integral windup (saturation while ∫e keeps growing) is the classic failure and is fixed by clamping/back-calculation. PID is the workhorse of robot joint and motor control because it needs no model.

entries.title: State-space representation
entries.summary: A linear time-invariant system is ẋ = A x + B u, y = C x + D u, where x is the internal state, u the input, y the output. A captures the autonomous dynamics, B how inputs push the state, C how state maps to measurement. This MIMO form supersedes single-input transfer functions and is the foundation for LQR, Kalman filtering, and pole placement — the basis of modern robot control.

entries.title: Controllability
entries.summary: A system is controllable if any state can be reached from any other in finite time using the input. For ẋ=Ax+Bu the test is the controllability matrix C=[B, AB, A²B, …, Aⁿ⁻¹B] having full rank n. If a mode isn't controllable, no feedback can move it — a structural limit on what a robot's actuators can achieve, independent of control law.

entries.title: Observability
entries.summary: A system is observable if its full internal state can be reconstructed from the output history. The test is the observability matrix O=[C; CA; CA²; …; CAⁿ⁻¹] having full rank n. Observability is the mathematical dual of controllability (O of (A,C) ↔ C of (Aᵀ,Cᵀ)) and is the precondition for any state estimator (e.g. a Kalman filter) to work on a robot's sensors.

entries.title: Lyapunov stability
entries.summary: Lyapunov's direct method proves stability WITHOUT solving the dynamics: find a scalar 'energy' V(x) with V(0)=0, V(x)>0 elsewhere, and V̇(x)≤0 along trajectories. Then the equilibrium is stable; V̇<0 gives asymptotic stability. It is the central tool for nonlinear and robot dynamics where closed-form solutions don't exist, and it underwrites control-Lyapunov-function design.

entries.title: LQR — Linear Quadratic Regulator
entries.summary: LQR designs the optimal linear state-feedback u=−Kx that minimizes the quadratic cost ∫(xᵀQx + uᵀRu)dt — Q penalizes state error, R penalizes control effort. The gain K=R⁻¹BᵀP comes from the solution P of the algebraic Riccati equation. LQR gives a principled, provably stabilizing controller and is the linear backbone of robot trajectory tracking and balancing.

entries.title: Kalman filter
entries.summary: The Kalman filter is the optimal recursive state estimator for a linear system under Gaussian process and measurement noise. It alternates predict (propagate state + covariance through the dynamics) and update (correct with the measurement, weighted by the Kalman gain that trades model vs sensor trust). It is the canonical sensor-fusion algorithm in robotics (IMU+GPS+odometry) and the bridge to probabilistic ML state estimation; the EKF/UKF extend it to nonlinear systems.

entries.title: Transfer function, poles & zeros
entries.summary: A transfer function G(s)=Y(s)/U(s) is the Laplace-domain input/output ratio of a linear system. Its poles (denominator roots) set stability and the speed/oscillation of the response — all poles in the open left-half plane ⇔ stable; its zeros shape transient behavior. Poles/zeros are the frequency-domain lens on the same dynamics state-space captures in time.

entries.title: Root locus
entries.summary: The root locus plots how the closed-loop poles migrate in the complex plane as a scalar gain K sweeps 0→∞. It shows graphically when increasing gain pushes poles into the right-half plane (instability) or toward desirable damping, making it a classic feedback-gain design tool that complements LQR's optimization view.

entries.title: Frequency response & stability margins
entries.summary: The Bode plot shows a system's gain and phase versus frequency. Gain margin (extra gain before instability) and phase margin (extra phase lag before instability) quantify robustness — how much the real plant can deviate from the model before feedback destabilizes it. Healthy margins (e.g. PM>45°) are why a controller tuned in simulation survives a real, uncertain robot.

entries.title: Pole placement
entries.summary: If a system is controllable, full-state feedback u=−Kx can place the closed-loop poles (eigenvalues of A−BK) anywhere you choose, directly setting the response's speed and damping; Ackermann's formula computes K for a single input. Pole placement is the design counterpart to LQR — you specify the poles instead of a cost — and requires the controllability condition to hold.

entries.title: Nyquist stability criterion
entries.summary: The Nyquist criterion judges closed-loop stability from the open-loop frequency response's encirclements of the −1 point: Z = N + P, where Z is unstable closed-loop poles, N the clockwise encirclements of −1, and P the open-loop right-half-plane poles. It handles time delays and non-minimum-phase systems that root locus struggles with — common in real robot actuation.

entries.title: Feedback linearization
entries.summary: Feedback linearization cancels a system's nonlinearity with state feedback so the closed loop behaves like a linear system, which can then be controlled by standard linear methods. For robot manipulators this is computed-torque control: u = M(q)v + C(q,q̇)q̇ + g(q) turns the nonlinear rigid-body dynamics into n decoupled double integrators. It requires an accurate model — its weakness.

entries.title: Model Predictive Control (MPC)
entries.summary: MPC solves a constrained optimization over a finite future horizon at every timestep, applies only the first control, then re-solves (receding horizon). Its decisive advantage is handling state/input CONSTRAINTS explicitly (actuator limits, obstacle/keep-out, safety bounds) — which LQR cannot. It is the modern method for legged locomotion, autonomous driving, and any robot operating near its limits, at the cost of solving an optimization in real time.

entries.title: Adaptive control
entries.summary: Adaptive control adjusts the controller online as it estimates unknown or changing plant parameters. Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) tunes gains so the closed loop tracks a reference model; the adaptation law is typically derived from a Lyapunov function to guarantee stability while learning. It handles a robot picking up an unknown payload — uncertainty that fixed-gain control can't.

entries.title: Sliding mode control
entries.summary: Sliding mode control forces the state onto a chosen sliding surface s(x)=0 and keeps it there with a high-frequency switching law, making the response robust to matched disturbances and model error. The trade-off is chattering (the switching excites unmodeled dynamics), mitigated by boundary-layer/super-twisting variants. It's favored where robustness beats smoothness.

entries.title: Optimal control ↔ reinforcement learning
entries.summary: Optimal control and reinforcement learning are the same problem in two languages. The Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman (HJB) PDE of optimal control is the continuous-time Bellman equation of RL; the value function is the cost-to-go; LQR is exactly the linear-quadratic case whose value function is the Riccati P. RL learns this value/policy from sampled experience when the model is unknown, where classical control solves it from the model — the bridge that lets robotics borrow deep RL.

entries.title: System identification
entries.summary: System identification fits a dynamics model (ẋ=f(x,u)) to measured input/output data — the data-driven counterpart to deriving equations from physics. Methods span least-squares ARX/state-space fitting to modern neural and Koopman-operator models. It is the formal link from control theory to ML: the learned model feeds MPC/LQR, and the quality of identification bounds the achievable control performance.
